narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 43.6% chance that you would fail—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and LSAT score of 32, both notably below the averages of 3.24 and 37.27 seen among students who passed the bar. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3 also placed you lower than the typical 5.91 decile for successful bar takers, while your middle family income bracket aligns more closely with the distribution among passers, where it's common at 35.3%. Among the factors the model weighed most heavily, your LSAT score stood out as the top influence, working strongly in your favor by suggesting a better-than-expected potential for success despite other challenges. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile, which pushed the prediction toward failure because it signals performance lagging behind peers who typically pass. Your family income level followed as the third key factor, contributing positively by matching a bracket where many bar passers come from. Overall, these elements tipped the balance toward rejection, even as your choice to pursue full-time studies aligns well with the 93.2% of passers in that category. Compared to admitted students, your profile shows strengths in commitment and background but gaps in core metrics like test scores and later-year rankings that the model prioritizes heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—many with similar starting points improve dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your upper-level grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and exploring programs with bridge support for bar preparation. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience could also help reframe your application for other schools. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort, and reapplying after addressing these areas often leads to success. Keep pushing; your full-time dedication is already a great foundation.
